claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

The first thing I need to know is about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you're claiming for multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh, hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children actually - Alex is 6 years old and Blake is 3 years old. Both are under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful! Thank you for that information. Since both Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old) are under 16, they both automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Let me continue with the next step of the assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about whether the children are working. This is mainly relevant for older children, but I need to ask for both:

**Are Alex and Blake both working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (6 and 3), I expect they're not working - but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not working at all - they're just little ones! Alex is in primary school and Blake is still a toddler.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I thought! Since Alex and Blake are both young children not in paid work, they meet this requirement. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! The next question is about benefits:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, this refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that would be claimed by the child themselves (not benefits you receive for them).

Given their ages, I'd expect they're not receiving any such benefits - can you confirm this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name. They're too young for that sort of thing.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The next question is about apprenticeships:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT in any apprenticeship programmes?**

Given their ages (6 and 3), I'm confident they're not in any apprenticeships - but just to confirm, neither child is in an apprenticeship, correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, definitely not - they're far too young for apprenticeships! Alex is just in primary school and Blake is still a toddler.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely! Let me move on to the next step of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. You can qualify through **any one** of the following paths:

**Do you meet any of these UK residency requirements?**

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident living in the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can support yourself financially (and not claiming certain benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant

Do any of these apply to you? For most people, it's simply that they normally live in the UK.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester with my family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check that you're responsible for Alex and Blake. You can qualify through **any one** of these:

**Are you responsible for the children in any of these ways?**

1. **Living with the children** - Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** - You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, both Alex and Blake live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since both children live with you, you clearly meet the responsibility requirement. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example, this could be another parent, a former partner, or another family member. 

- If **no one else is claiming**, we can proceed
- If **someone else is claiming but you've agreed** that you should be the one to claim instead, we can also proceed
- If **there's a dispute** about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision

What's your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that keeps things straightforward. Let me continue to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about where Alex and Blake are living:

**Are both children currently in a normal living situation at home with you?**

Or is either of them:
- In hospital
- In local authority care
- Being fostered

What's the situation for Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex is at home with me, no issues there. But Blake... Blake has been in hospital for a few months now, actually.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Blake has been in hospital - I hope they're doing okay.

Since Alex and Blake are in different situations, I'll need to check them separately. Let me first continue with Blake's situation as there are special rules about hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. There are special rules for children in hospital.

**How long has Blake been in hospital?**

Here's what you need to know:
- Child Benefit **continues for up to 12 weeks** while a child is in hospital
- If Blake has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks cumulatively, eligibility may be affected

Also, there's a "linking rule": if a child leaves hospital and returns within 28 days, those stays are added together towards the 12-week limit.

Could you tell me approximately how long Blake has been in hospital? Is it:
- **Under 12 weeks** (less than about 3 months)
- **Over 12 weeks** (more than about 3 months)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been more than 3 months now - I'd say about 13 weeks or so.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for sharing that. Since Blake has been in hospital for about 13 weeks, which is over the 12-week limit, there's an important follow-up question:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question - there's an exception to the 12-week rule:

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake while they're in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Buying clothes, toys, or books for them
- Bringing food or treats during visits
- Paying for any items or comforts for them
- Other regular expenses for Blake's benefit

If you (or your partner, if you're married/civil partners living together) are regularly spending money on Blake, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit even though they've been in hospital for more than 12 weeks.

Are you regularly spending money on Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand - hospital stays can be financially difficult for families.

Unfortunately, this means that for Blake, since they've been in hospital for over 12 weeks and you're not currently spending money regularly on them, you wouldn't be eligible for Child Benefit for Blake at this time.

However, let me now check Alex's situation, since Alex is at home with you in a normal living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for Alex, who is living at home with you. There's one final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, Alex is just living at home with you normally, and the council isn't paying for their care or housing - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, the council isn't paying anything for Alex. They just live at home with me normally.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I now have all the information I need to give you my assessment for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Results

Thank you for going through the assessment with me. Here's the outcome for your family:

---

### ✅ Alex (age 6) - **ELIGIBLE**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. They meet all the requirements:
- Under 16 years old
- Living at home with you in Manchester
- No one else is claiming for them
- No council payments towards their care

---

### ❌ Blake (age 3) - **NOT ELIGIBLE** (currently)

I'm sorry, but you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Blake because:
- Blake has been in hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit
- The exception (regularly spending money on Blake while in hospital) doesn't apply in your situation

**Important:** This could change if:
- Blake comes home from hospital, or
- You start regularly spending money on Blake (even small amounts for clothes, toys, treats, etc.)

If either of these situations changes, you may be able to claim again - contact HMRC to discuss.

---

### Next Steps for Alex:

1. **Apply online** or by post using form CH2
2. **Provide** proof of your identity and Alex's birth certificate
3. **Be aware** that if you or a partner earns over £60,000, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply
4. **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

🔗 More information: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

---

I'm really sorry to hear about Blake's hospital stay. I hope they recover soon, and please do contact HMRC if your circumstances change. Is there anything else I can help you with?

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit: (1) Under 16 years old - qualifies automatically for age requirement, (2) Not working - too young for paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living at home in a normal living situation, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit at this time. While Blake meets the basic eligibility criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK, claimant is responsible), Blake has been in hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. The exception to this rule would apply if the claimant was regularly spending money on Blake while in hospital, but the claimant confirmed they have not been able to afford to do so. Under these circumstances, Child Benefit eligibility for Blake stops after the 12-week hospital period.
arguments.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex (6) is fully eligible for Child Benefit as they meet all standard requirements and live at home normally. Blake (3) is currently ineligible because they have been in hospital for over 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks) and the claimant is not regularly spending money on them during this time. If Blake returns home or if the claimant begins regularly spending money on Blake, eligibility could be reassessed.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit: (1) Under 16 years old - qualifies automatically for age requirement, (2) Not working - too young for paid work, (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Not in an apprenticeship, (5) Claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) Claimant is responsible for Alex as they live together, (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living at home in a normal living situation, and (9) The local council is not paying for Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is NOT eligible for Child Benefit at this time. While Blake meets the basic eligibility criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship, claimant lives in UK, claimant is responsible), Blake has been in hospital for approximately 13 weeks, which exceeds the 12-week limit. The exception to this rule would apply if the claimant was regularly spending money on Blake while in hospital, but the claimant confirmed they have not been able to afford to do so. Under these circumstances, Child Benefit eligibility for Blake stops after the 12-week hospital period.

response.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex (6) is fully eligible for Child Benefit as they meet all standard requirements and live at home normally. Blake (3) is currently ineligible because they have been in hospital for over 12 weeks (approximately 13 weeks) and the claimant is not regularly spending money on them during this time. If Blake returns home or if the claimant begins regularly spending money on Blake, eligibility could be reassessed.
